From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Figure skater

Horatio Torromé

| Personal information | |
| --- | --- |
| Full name | Horatio Tertuliano Torromé |
| Other names | Henri Torromé |
| Born | 1861  [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") |
| Died | 16 September 1920 (aged 58–59)  [Willesden](/wiki/Willesden "Willesden"), [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London") |
| Figure skating career | |
|  | |
| Country | [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")    [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") |
| Skating club | Prince's Skating Club, GBR |
|  | |

**Horatio Tertuliano Torromé**, also credited as **Henri Torromé** (1861 – 16 September 1920), was a British [figure skater](/wiki/Figure_skater "Figure skater") who also competed for Argentina.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Torromé was born in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") to an Argentine father and a Brazilian mother, and not long after his birth, the family emigrated to London, England. He took up [ice skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating") at his local club, which was the [Prince's Skating Club](/wiki/Prince%27s_Skating_Club "Prince's Skating Club") in [Knightsbridge](/wiki/Knightsbridge "Knightsbridge"), London.[[2]](#cite_note-Bio-2) Torromé competed at the [1902 World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/1902_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1902 World Figure Skating Championships") in London, and he finished in fourth place out of four starters.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In 1905 and 1906, he came first at the [British Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/British_Figure_Skating_Championships "British Figure Skating Championships").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Although Torromé qualified to represent Great Britain at the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics"), he opted to compete for his father's home country and so became the only representative for Argentina at the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics") held in London. He scored 228.9 points in the [men's individual competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics – Men's singles"), placing last of the seven skaters who finished competition.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Torromé also served as a judge for the [pairs competition](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Pairs "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics – Pairs") at the same Games.[[6]](#cite_note-Judge-6) He would continue to be an active judge and was a part of the judging team for the [1912 World Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/1912_World_Figure_Skating_Championships "1912 World Figure Skating Championships").[[2]](#cite_note-Bio-2)

From 1881, Torromé was a partner in his father's Commissioned Merchants business in Laurence Pountney Lane, [Candlewick](/wiki/Candlewick_(ward) "Candlewick (ward)"), London. His father retired in 1903 and left the business to be run by Horatio and his brother Franco. Torromé was also known for being an artist.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Horatio Torromé died on 16 September 1920 in [Willesden](/wiki/Willesden "Willesden"), London.[[2]](#cite_note-Bio-2)
